findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the plan's lens enumeration from 'three lenses (agent-process / gate-economics / code-health)' to the actual four lenses found in the codebase: `agent_process`, `gate_economics`, `code_health`, and `delivery` — and update AC2's grep assertion to match.
evidence: Plan: 'documenting the `rebar metrics` command, the three lenses (agent-process / gate-economics / code-health)'
src/rebar/metrics/registry.py line 152: `lens="delivery"` on `commit_cadence_trend`
src/rebar/metrics/event_metrics.py line 347: `lens="agent_process"`
src/rebar/metrics/sidecar_metrics.py line 269: `lens="gate_economics"`
src/rebar/metrics/git_metrics.py line 256: `lens="code_health"`
Four distinct lens values exist: agent_process, gate_economics, code_health, delivery
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'three lenses — agent-process, gate-economics, code-health' in both the Files section and AC2, but the actual registry has a fourth lens value: `delivery` (used by `commit_cadence_trend` in registry.py line 152). The user-guide Metrics section and discovery.md addition will be factually wrong if they enumerate only three lenses.
findings.location: Files section (`docs/user-guide.md` bullet); AC2 ('three lenses (agent-process / gate-economics / code-health)')
scenarios: A contributor or agent reads the Metrics section in user-guide.md, enumerates 'three lenses', then encounters a `delivery`-lens metric in JSON output and cannot reconcile the discrepancy.
The AC2 grep/test that checks for the lens names will either miss `delivery` (leaving the doc stale) or fail because the doc only lists three.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Files bullet for `docs/user-guide.md` and in AC2, replace 'the three lenses (agent-process / gate-economics / code-health)' with 'the four lenses (agent-process / gate-economics / code-health / delivery)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the non-existent metric references ('complexity/clone/coverage trend') in the discovery.md guidance and What/Files description with the actual registered code-health metric IDs: `module_size_distribution`, `churn`, `module_size_trend`, and `oversized_module_count`.

Search for 'complexity' in src/rebar/metrics: no matches in metric registrations
findings.finding: The plan instructs the discovery.md addition to have a subagent cite 'complexity/clone/coverage trend' as `rebar metrics` output values, but no `complexity`, `clone`, or `coverage` metrics exist in the REGISTRY or any registered metric module. The actual code-health metrics are `module_size_distribution`, `churn`, `module_size_trend`, and `oversized_module_count`. Citing non-existent metric IDs as evidence will mislead janitor subagents.
findings.location: Files section (`examples/agent-skills/rebar-janitor/phases/discovery.md` bullet); What section ('complexity/clone/coverage movement over a date range')
scenarios: A janitor subagent following discovery.md looks for `complexity` and `clone` values in `rebar metrics --output json` output and finds none, then either incorrectly reports the metrics as all `unavailable` or treats the absence as zero — both wrong.
The doc ships describing metrics that the command never emits, reducing trust in the whole Metrics section.
findings.suggested_fix: In the discovery.md Files bullet, change 'complexity/clone/coverage trend, churn / refactor-to-addition ratio' to 'module-size distribution (`module_size_distribution`), oversized-module count (`oversized_module_count`), and churn (`churn`)' to match the actual registry.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the lens names throughout the plan (What, Files/user-guide.md description, and AC2) to use the actual underscore identifiers from the code (`agent_process`, `gate_economics`, `code_health`, `delivery`) and enumerate all four registered lenses, not three.

Plan's AC2 asserts the user-guide section names 'the three lenses (agent-process / gate-economics / code-health)' — omits delivery and misspells lens ids
findings.finding: The plan names the metric lenses as 'agent-process / gate-economics / code-health' (three, hyphenated), but the landed code registers four lenses with underscore identifiers: `agent_process` (event_metrics.py:347), `gate_economics` (sidecar_metrics.py:269), `code_health` (registry.py:136,144 and git_metrics.py:256), and `delivery` (registry.py:152). The plan omits `delivery` entirely and uses hyphenated forms that don't match the JSON output field values.
findings.location: ## What / ## Files / docs/user-guide.md section / AC line 2
scenarios: The docs/user-guide.md Metrics section is authored using the plan's lens names, producing documentation that names three lenses with hyphens — when an agent or user filters rebar metrics --output json by lens field, they will find 'agent_process', 'gate_economics', 'code_health', and 'delivery', not the documented hyphenated names.
The AC2 grep test asserting 'the three lenses' will pass against incorrect documentation, locking in the error.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'three lenses — agent-process, gate-economics, code-health' with 'four lenses — `agent_process`, `gate_economics`, `code_health`, `delivery`' everywhere the plan lists them (What section, Files/user-guide.md bullet, AC2 grep assertion).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the non-existent 'complexity/clone/coverage trend' citation instruction in `discovery.md` and `docs/user-guide.md` with the actual registered code-health metrics (`module_size_distribution`, `churn`, `refactor_to_addition_ratio`, `cap_change_events`) so agents cite real metric IDs.
evidence: Plan: 'cite code-health-lens metric values (module-size distribution vs the 800 cap, complexity/clone/coverage trend, churn / refactor-to-addition ratio) as evidence'
src/rebar/metrics/git_metrics.py registers: module_size_distribution, churn, refactor_to_addition_ratio, cap_change_events — no complexity, no clone, no coverage
src/rebar/metrics/sidecar_metrics.py registers: cost_per_accepted_change, env_diagnosis_intervals, first_pass_verification (all gate_economics lens)
Coverage exists only as a parse helper in src/rebar/metrics/adapters/github_actions.py, not a registered REGISTRY metric
findings.finding: The plan instructs the janitor skill to cite 'complexity/clone/coverage trend' from `rebar metrics`, but no complexity, clone, or coverage metrics exist in the actual REGISTRY — the real registered code-health metrics are `module_size_distribution`, `churn`, `refactor_to_addition_ratio`, and `cap_change_events`. An agent following this guidance will either hallucinate metric values or fail to locate the evidence it was told to cite.
scenarios: A janitor Discovery subagent runs `rebar metrics --output json`, finds no 'complexity', 'clone', or 'coverage' keys in the output, and either flags an error or invents values — neither of which produces grounded evidence.
A documentation reader consulting `docs/user-guide.md`'s Metrics section sees 'complexity/clone/coverage' as a named lens capability and writes tooling against metric IDs that do not exist.
findings.suggested_fix: In discovery.md and user-guide.md, replace 'complexity/clone/coverage trend' with the real metric IDs: module-size distribution (`module_size_distribution`), churn (`churn`), refactor-to-addition ratio (`refactor_to_addition_ratio`), and cap-change events (`cap_change_events`).

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'three lenses — agent-process / gate-economics / code-health' as the canonical taxonomy, but the shipped registry actually registers FOUR distinct lens values: `agent_process`, `gate_economics`, `code_health`, and `delivery`. The `delivery` lens (from `registry.py`'s `commit_cadence_trend` spec) is unaccounted for. If user-guide.md and discovery.md are authored from this spec they will document a three-lens surface that misrepresents actual `rebar metrics --output json` output.
findings.location: ## What / ## Files / docs/user-guide.md section / AC line 2 — 'three lenses (agent-process / gate-economics / code-health)'
scenarios: A contributor reads user-guide.md and queries `rebar metrics --output json`; they see a `delivery` lens entry that the doc never mentions, creating confusion about whether the command is working correctly.
The rebar-janitor discovery subagent is told there are 'three lenses' and ignores or misclassifies `delivery`-lens metrics like commit_cadence_trend when citing evidence.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'three lenses (agent-process / gate-economics / code-health)' with 'four lenses (agent-process / gate-economics / code-health / delivery)' everywhere in the plan (## What, ## Files for user-guide.md, AC line 2). Confirm by running `python -c "import rebar.metrics; print({s.lens for s in rebar.metrics.REGISTRY})"`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Commit to a single, mandatory test location for ACs 1–4 (e.g., a new `tests/scripts/check_metrics_docs.sh` or named additions to an existing test file) and change 'can be' to 'must be' in the Tests section so the executor cannot omit the verification step.
evidence: 'These can be a lightweight `tests/scripts/`-tier grep test or folded into the existing docs-index/README drift checks.' — 'can be' and 'or' are non-committal
AC1: 'verified by a grep/test asserting the `rebar metrics` reference ... are present' — assumes a test will exist but no step mandates its creation
AC2/3/4: each 'asserted by a grep/test' without naming an existing test that already covers these assertions
findings.finding: The Tests section says the verification grep tests 'can be a lightweight `tests/scripts/`-tier grep test OR folded into the existing docs-index/README drift checks,' leaving it ambiguous whether a new test file must be created. ACs 1–4 each say 'verified by a grep/test' but since the plan never mandates creating these tests, an executor could ship the doc edits without adding any test, leaving the ACs with no in-repo proving command.
findings.location: Tests section and Acceptance Criteria AC1–AC4
scenarios: Executor ships only the four doc/skill edits, skips the test creation step because the plan says it 'can be' done; ACs 1–4 are satisfied by human review but no automated proving command exists; a future regression is undetected.
Executor adds a grep test for some ACs but not others because the 'or folded into existing checks' path is ambiguous about whether existing drift gates cover the new assertions.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the ambiguous Tests paragraph with: 'Create `tests/scripts/check_metrics_docs.sh` (or extend `tests/scripts/check_docs_index.sh` if it exists) with four `grep -qF` assertions — one per AC target file/string — and add it to the CI docs-check job. Each AC's proving command is then `bash tests/scripts/check_metrics_docs.sh`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC2's grep/test assertion to also check for the `source`/`confidence` labels and the `unavailable` state keyword, so the verify step covers all content the criterion requires.
evidence: AC2 text: 'docs/user-guide.md contains a "Metrics" section documenting the rebar metrics command, the three lenses (agent-process / gate-economics / code-health), and the source+confidence labels + unavailable state'
AC2 verify clause: 'asserted by a grep/test for the section heading and the lens names'
The verify step omits any assertion for source/confidence labels or the unavailable state, which are named as required content in the same criterion.
findings.finding: AC2's verify step is narrower than its own requirement text: AC2 states the section must document 'source+confidence labels + unavailable state' but its grep assertion only checks 'the section heading and the lens names' — leaving source/confidence labels unverified.
scenarios: An implementer writes the section heading and lens names but omits source/confidence documentation; the grep test passes but the criterion's own stated requirement is unmet.
A reviewer checking AC2 completion finds the test passes while the substantive doc requirement is unfulfilled.
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC2's verify clause to: 'asserted by a grep/test for the section heading, the three lens names, the strings `source` and `confidence`, and `unavailable`.'

evidence: 2 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Tests section that the 'fold into existing docs-index checks' path only applies to the two docs/ ACs (user-guide.md § and reuse-surface.md reference); the discovery.md and AGENTS.md content-presence assertions need a separate lightweight grep test (tests/scripts/ tier or a new tests/unit/test_metrics_discoverability.py) because scripts/check_docs_index.py is scoped to docs/*.md only.
evidence: Plan Tests: 'a check that examples/agent-skills/rebar-janitor/phases/discovery.md mentions rebar metrics … These can be a lightweight tests/scripts/-tier grep test or folded into the existing docs-index/README drift checks.'
scripts/check_docs_index.py lines 36-50: find_unindexed() only reads docs/README.md and enumerates docs/*.md — no awareness of examples/ or root-level AGENTS.md
tests/unit/test_check_docs_index.py lines 3-6: 'keeps docs/README.md honest: every living top-level docs/*.md must be linked from the index' — scope is explicitly docs/ only
findings.finding: The Tests section proposes 'folding' the discovery.md and AGENTS.md grep-assertions into 'the existing docs-index/README drift checks,' but the existing docs-index gate (scripts/check_docs_index.py) only verifies that top-level docs/*.md files appear as links in docs/README.md and that relative markdown links within docs/ resolve — it cannot assert content presence inside examples/agent-skills/rebar-janitor/phases/discovery.md or AGENTS.md, which live outside docs/. Treating the docs-index gate as a catch-all for all four ACs is a golden-hammer misfit: two ACs are simply out of scope for that mechanism.
scenarios: An implementer attempts to satisfy ACs 1 and 3 by adding assertions inside test_check_docs_index.py, discovers the gate's data model has no path to examples/ or AGENTS.md, and must write separate tests anyway — wasting time on a false shortcut.
ACs 1 and 3 get skipped or go untested because the implementer assumes the existing gate handles them.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'or folded into the existing docs-index/README drift checks' with 'or folded into the existing docs-index checks for the two docs/ ACs; the discovery.md and AGENTS.md assertions need a separate tests/unit/ grep test (e.g. test_metrics_discoverability.py) since check_docs_index.py is scoped to docs/*.md only.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a command-failure fallback clause to `discovery.md`: if `rebar metrics` exits non-zero or returns unparseable JSON, the agent should fall back to cloc/tokei-only evidence, record the failure as a caveat in its findings, and not block Discovery.
evidence: Plan text: 'Note the `unavailable` state (a metric whose signal has not accrued yet) so the agent does not treat absence as zero.' — covers data-level absence but not command-level failure.
ABSENCE: no instruction for the subagent when `rebar metrics` exits non-zero or returns unparseable output.
Plan text: 'when the repo is a rebar store' — acknowledges conditionality but doesn't specify failure handling within that branch.
findings.finding: The discovery.md skill guidance specifies what to do when a metric value is `unavailable` (not yet accrued), but does not address what the subagent should do if the `rebar metrics` command itself fails (non-zero exit, malformed/empty JSON, or timeout) — leaving an autonomous agent without a fallback path.
scenarios: Subagent runs `rebar metrics --output json`, the command fails (binary missing, store corrupted, permission error), the agent receives no JSON and has no instruction — it may block, raise an unhandled error, or silently skip evidence without recording the gap.
Malformed JSON output (partial write, version mismatch) causes the agent to crash or misparse metrics as zeros rather than falling back to cloc/tokei-only evidence.
findings.suggested_fix: In the discovery.md guidance, after the `rebar metrics` invocation instruction, add: 'If the command fails or returns malformed output, fall back to the cloc/tokei/ast-grep snapshot evidence only; note in the Discovery report that trend data was unavailable due to a command error (include the exit code or error message as context).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a fallback rule to the discovery.md `rebar metrics` instruction mirroring the document's existing pattern: e.g. 'if the command fails or the repo is not a rebar store, skip and note the absence; do not treat a missing result as zero-valued metrics.'
evidence: Plan says to add rebar metrics invocation 'when the repo is a rebar store' but does not specify what to do when: (a) the repo is NOT a rebar store, (b) the rebar CLI is not installed, or (c) the command returns a non-zero exit code
Existing discovery.md line 14-15: 'If not and they'd help, offer to install them… Don't block — fall back to rg/grep' — established fallback pattern for tool absence
Existing discovery.md line 78: 'If history is shallow/squashed, say so and report what's computable' — established partial-data fallback pattern
The plan's AC1 only requires a grep asserting the rebar metrics reference and unavailable-state caveat are present — no fallback assertion is included
findings.finding: The plan's discovery.md addition instructs the subagent to run `rebar metrics --since <date> --until <date> --output json` but specifies no fallback for command failure (non-zero exit, command not found, not a rebar store, malformed date args). The existing discovery.md consistently provides degradation rules for every tool dependency ('Don't block — fall back to rg/grep'; 'If history is shallow/squashed, say so and report what's computable'). The new rebar metrics instruction breaks this established pattern, leaving the subagent with no protocol when the command fails.
findings.location: ## Files — `examples/agent-skills/rebar-janitor/phases/discovery.md` bullet; also the Approach section: 'when the repo is a rebar store'
scenarios: The target repo is not a rebar store; the subagent has no instruction for what to do and may block, hallucinate output, or silently skip the step without noting it.
The rebar CLI is installed but returns exit 2 (malformed date arguments); the subagent has no protocol to handle this and may misreport the evidence pool.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the rebar metrics guidance in discovery.md: 'If `rebar metrics` returns a non-zero exit or is unavailable (not a rebar store / CLI not installed), skip this step and note the absence in the evidence pool — do not treat missing output as zero-valued metrics.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive decision rule alongside the unavailable-state caveat in the discovery.md instruction: specify what the subagent should record in the evidence field when a metric is unavailable (e.g. 'cite the unavailable reason and accruing_since as a note; do not suppress the gap — record it as a finding that trend data has not yet accrued for <metric_id> since <accruing_since>').
evidence: Plan states: 'Note the unavailable{reason,accruing_since} state so the agent does not treat absence as zero' — a negative constraint only
src/rebar/metrics/registry.py:125-128: _no_data_yet returns None for all three seed metrics — meaning ALL current metrics will return unavailable, making this the dominant runtime case

discovery.md's output schema (lines 80-92) requires a 'evidence' field with 'path:line citation(s) and/or a metric'; no protocol is given for how to fill this field when the metric answer is 'unavailable' with a reason and accruing_since
Contrast: temporal decay section (lines 76-78) gives a concrete partial-data protocol: 'If history is shallow/squashed, say so and report what's computable'
findings.finding: The plan says the discovery.md addition should 'note the unavailable state so the agent does not treat absence as zero', but gives no co-located decision rule for what the subagent should do when the metrics output contains only unavailable entries (which is the current state of all three seed metrics, since every compute is _no_data_yet). The distinction 'do not treat as zero' is a negative constraint but the processing protocol — what evidence claim to make when all metrics are unavailable — is left unspecified.
findings.location: ## Files — `examples/agent-skills/rebar-janitor/phases/discovery.md` bullet; also 'Note the unavailable state'
scenarios: All seed metrics return unavailable (the current state). The subagent reads the plan's negative constraint ('don't treat as zero') but has no positive rule: should it cite the accruing_since date as evidence? Note the gap as an absence finding? Omit the evidence field? Different subagents will behave differently.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a lightweight test (or extend the existing docs-drift check) that asserts the flag names and key JSON field names cited in `discovery.md` appear in `rebar metrics --help` output (or the CLI spec), so the skill doc is mechanically verified against the shipped interface and kept in sync on future CLI changes.
evidence: Plan states 'it references the command's real, landed interface' — asserted, not verified
Hardcoded flags: `rebar metrics --since <date> --until <date> --output json`
Hardcoded field names: `unavailable{reason,accruing_since}`, 'module-size distribution vs the 800 cap', 'churn / refactor-to-addition ratio'
No sync check in Tests or AC: AC only grep-asserts the text exists, not that the text matches the shipped interface
No stated process (e.g., a docs-drift test comparing discovery.md flag names against the CLI --help output) for future interface changes
findings.finding: The discovery.md addition hard-codes the CLI invocation `rebar metrics --since <date> --until <date> --output json` and references specific output fields (module-size distribution, complexity/clone/coverage, `unavailable{reason,accruing_since}`) drawn from the 9a5a landing, but the plan provides no mechanism to verify the flag syntax/JSON schema actually matches what shipped, nor any stated owner or process for keeping the skill doc current if the CLI interface evolves — creating a stale-reference risk across a component boundary.
findings.location: Files section (discovery.md addition) and Approach ('references the command's real, landed interface (and so docs/cli-reference.md already lists the subcommand)')
scenarios: A contributor renames a flag or restructures the JSON output in a future rebar metrics iteration; the janitor skill silently references a stale invocation and agents produce broken tool calls
The `unavailable` state shape changes; discovery.md still documents the old fields and agents misinterpret absence of the new field
findings.suggested_fix: In the Tests section, add: 'a grep/diff test that extracts flag names from `discovery.md`'s `rebar metrics` invocation and asserts they match those in `docs/cli-reference.md`'s metrics entry (or `rebar metrics --help` output captured as a fixture).' This closes the loop between the skill doc and the real interface without requiring runtime execution in CI.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either (a) update the parent epic (86ac) to enumerate a fourth 'Discoverability' track covering the janitor-skill and docs deliverables — which re-opens the parent's plan-review attestation and requires re-review — or (b) re-parent this leaf to a more appropriate epic whose scope explicitly includes documentation and agent-skill updates for the metrics surface.
evidence: Parent Decomposition: 'Three tracks; each child is a single concern (detail lives in the children): Command … Instrumentation … Backfill …'

Parent ACs enumerate: epic rollup, rebar metrics CLI correctness, is_authoritative segregation, bug-close friction, backfill validation — none mention janitor skill, user-guide Metrics section, reuse-surface.md, or AGENTS.md.

Leaf What: 'Two deliverables: (1) teach the rebar-janitor skill … (2) reference the metrics surface from the project's canonical documentation' — neither deliverable appears in any of the parent's three tracks.
Leaf Approach: 'Documentation- and skill-only; no src/rebar change' — confirms this is a distinct track not enumerated in the parent.
findings.finding: The leaf introduces a fourth deliverable track ('discoverability/docs + rebar-janitor skill') that is absent from the parent epic's explicit three-track decomposition (Command / Instrumentation / Backfill) and from all five of the parent's Acceptance Criteria. While the leaf's goal is consistent with the parent's top-level 'usable' framing, the parent's Decomposition section says 'Three tracks; each child is a single concern (detail lives in the children)' — implying the decomposition is complete and enumerated. The janitor-skill update (discovery.md), user-guide Metrics section, reuse-surface.md reference, and AGENTS.md pointer are all outside any named track.
findings.location: Leaf plan: ## What / ## Files; Parent epic 86ac-086a-20ad-4327: ## Decomposition and ## Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: An implementer reading the parent epic to understand what children are expected finds no mandate for janitor-skill or docs updates, raising the question of whether this leaf was intended to be a child of a different (perhaps future) epic or whether the parent needs updating to enumerate a fourth 'Discoverability' track.

If the epic's child-rollup AC ('rebar list --epic <this> --status open returning none') is used to declare the epic done, this leaf's closure could be counted toward that without the parent having explicitly contracted the work — creating a phantom scope expansion.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Discoverability' track paragraph to the parent epic's ## Decomposition section: 'Discoverability: update `examples/agent-skills/rebar-janitor/phases/discovery.md` to consult `rebar metrics`, add a Metrics section to `docs/user-guide.md`, reference `rebar.metrics` in `docs/reuse-surface.md`, and add a pointer in `AGENTS.md`.' Then add a corresponding AC asserting the janitor skill and docs sections are present (grep-verifiable). This makes the parent's scope explicit and keeps the leaf a proper subset.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
